title: Commands & Requests
description: Ask, tell, and request — from gentle to firm — the way Delhi people actually do it.
culturalNote: Hindi has multiple levels of command that signal your relationship. 'Karo' (informal), 'kariye' (polite), 'karo na' (softened with 'na'). In Delhi, adding 'zaraa' or 'please' softens a command into a request. Getting this register right matters — using the wrong level sounds either rude or overly stiff.

exercises.note: 'Bolo' = say/tell (informal). 'Sach bolo' = tell the truth. 'Kuch bolo na' = say something, won't you? 'Bolo yaar' = come on, say it. Formal: 'boliye'. Very common in Delhi — 'haan bolo' = yes, go on/say it.

'Suno' = listen/hey (to get attention). 'Suno, yeh important hai' = listen, this is important. 'Suno na' = hey listen. Also used like 'hey' to call someone: just 'Suno!' to get a friend's attention. Formal: 'suniye' — used to call waiters, shopkeepers.
'Aao' = come (informal). 'Ghar aao' = come home. 'Jaldi aao' = come quickly. Formal: 'aaiye'. 'Aao na' = come on, come (softer, a bit pleading). 'Aate raho' = keep coming/stay in touch.

exercises.prompt: You want to politely get a waiter's attention and say 'please listen.' What do you say?
exercises.hint: Formal command for 'listen' — use with strangers/elders

exercises.note: 'Mat' = don't (for commands). 'Yeh mat karo' = don't do this. 'Tension mat lo' = don't stress. 'Ro mat' = don't cry. 'Bhool mat jaana' = don't forget. Always use 'mat' (not 'nahi') for negative commands. 'Nahi' is for statements.

'Zaraa' turns commands into gentle requests. 'Zaraa dekho' = just take a look. 'Zaraa ruko' = just wait a moment. 'Zaraa bata' = just tell me. 'Zaraa' is the magic word that makes a command sound polite without using formal forms. Use it constantly.

exercises.sentenceEnglish: Don't do this — it's very important.

exercises.note: 'Ruko' = wait/stop (informal). 'Ek minute ruko' = wait one minute. 'Ruko na' = wait, won't you? Formal: 'rukiye'. 'Ruk' (very informal/abrupt) = stop. 'Ruk ja' = stop/hold on. 'Rukte ho?' = do you stop here? (asking a rickshaw driver)

'Dekho' = look (informal). 'Dekho na' = look, please. 'Dekho, main sahi hoon' = look, I'm right (argumentative). 'Zaraa dekho' = just take a look. Also used to say 'you see' or 'look here' in an argument: 'Dekho, aise nahi hoga' = look, this won't work like this.

exercises.prompt: Your friend is worried about an exam. What's the most natural comforting command?

exercises.note: 'Jaao' = go (informal). 'Ghar jaao' = go home. 'Jaldi jaao' = go quickly. 'Jaao na yaar' = come on, go! 'Jaane do' = let it go/let them go (accepting something). 'Jaane de' = forget it (dismissive, moving on).

exercises.sentenceEnglish: Just sit, I'll be right back.

exercises.instruction: Match the command to its meaning

exercises.prompt: Which sentence most naturally means 'Just tell me what happened'?
